title: Wet-felted wool dryer balls
subtitle: A set of four dense wool balls for tumble dryer use instead of dryer sheets
excerpt: Wool dryer balls reduce drying time by separating laundry in the drum and absorbing then releasing moisture as the cycle runs. They replace synthetic dryer sheets and last for several years. Making a set of four requires dense, fully-felted balls that will not shed fibres in the wash. Superwash wools will not felt; use untreated wool roving.
sourceNotes: Wool dryer ball technique is widely established in modern craft instruction. The underlying wet-felting mechanism is traditional.

content.text: Each ball starts as a wound core of roving, similar to a ball of yarn, then felts in the washing machine. The machine cycle provides the agitation; the trick is winding the core tightly enough that the ball keeps its round shape during felting.
will not work. Check the roving label: wool labelled machine-washable or treated will not felt. British rare-breed wools (Herdwick, Shetland, Jacob) felt readily and make particularly dense balls.

attrs.body: These balls are machine-washed at 60°C or higher. Keep other laundry out of the machine for the first wash cycle as loose fibres may transfer.
attrs.title: Hot machine wash

items.qty: 200 g per ball (800 g total for 4 balls)
items.name: Untreated wool roving (not superwash)
Old nylon tights or mesh laundry bag

content.text: Take a length of roving and wind it tightly around your first two fingers about 10 times. Slip the coil off and fold it in half. Begin winding the remaining roving around the folded coil in all directions, rotating the bundle after every few wraps to keep the shape round. Wind until the ball is about 9 cm in diameter (it will shrink to approximately 7 cm after felting).
Step 2: Secure and tuck the end

When the final layer is in place, tuck the roving tail under several wraps using your finger. The ball should feel firm and compact. Squeeze it: it should spring back. If it deforms and stays deformed, the layers are too loose: wind more roving over the top.

Drop the first ball into the toe of a nylon stocking. Tie a firm knot above the ball. Place the second ball above the knot, knot again. Repeat for all four balls. The tights hold each ball as a separate unit in the machine so they do not press against each other and lose their round shape.

Run the tights through a full 60°C wash cycle with a small amount of laundry detergent. The agitation and heat causes the fibres to mat and felt. After the cycle, cut open the tights and check the balls. If they are firm and
, they are ready to dry. If they still feel spongy, re-wrap each ball in a fresh knot of tights and run a second cycle.

Dry the balls either in the tumble dryer or flat in a warm place. Once dry, use three or four balls per dryer load. They absorb and release moisture through the cycle, reducing drying time for a typical load by 15 to 25 per cent.

attrs.intro: Two common failures with dryer balls.

items.fix: Check the roving for superwash treatment. If the wool is correct, run the second cycle with the tightest machine setting available.
items.cause: Roving is superwash-treated or the machine temperature did not reach 60°C.
items.symptom: Balls did not felt after one machine cycle

items.fix: Each ball must be knotted separately inside the tights so it cannot press flat against another ball during agitation.
items.cause: Not secured individually in the tights.
items.symptom: Balls lost their round shape and became flat on one side

attrs.heading: Common problems
